The Handen-Shūju regulated land ownership. Based on the registration, each citizen over 6 was entitled to a {{nihongo|"distributed field"|口分田|kubunden}}, subject to taxation (approx. 3% of crops). The area of each field was 2 {{nihongo|''tan''|段}} for men (approx. 22 ares total), and two-thirds of this amount for women. (However, the [[:en:Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō|Shinuhi and Kenin castes]] were only entitled to 1/3 of this area). The field was returned to the country at death. Land belonging to shrines and temples was exempt from taxation. Collection and redistribution of land took place every 6 years. ===Castes=== {{main|Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō}} The population was divided in two castes, ''Ryōmin'' (良民) (furthermore divided into 4 sub-castes{{Citation needed|date=December 2007}}) and ''Senmin'' (賤民) (divided into 5 sub-castes), the latter being close to slaves. Citizens wore different colors according to their caste. ==Evolution of Ritsuryō application== Several modifications were added over time. In order to promote cultivation, a law allowing the ownership for three generations of newly arable fields was promulgated in 723 (三世一身の法, ''Sanze-isshin Law'') and then without limits in 743 (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Law''). This led to the appearance of large private lands, the first ''[[shōen]]s''. Strict application of the Handen-Shūju system decayed in the 8th and 9th century.{{Cite book|title=A history of Japan|last=D.|first=Totman, Conrad|date=2000-01-01|publisher=Blackwell Publishers|isbn=1557860769|pages=100|oclc=41967280}} In an attempt to maintain the system, the period between each collection/distribution was extended to 12 years under [[Emperor Kanmu]]. At the beginning of [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the system was almost not enforced. The last collection/distribution took place between 902 and 903. The caste system was less and less strictly enforced. Some Ryōmin would wed Senmin to avoid taxation, and Senmin/Ryōmin children would become Ryōmin. At the end of the 9th century / beginning of the 10th, the caste system was practically void of its substance. Hereditary high-Ranks for public posts led to the monopoly of occupation of the most important posts by a limited number of families, in effect a nobility, amongst which the [[Fujiwara clan]], [[Minamoto clan]], [[Taira clan]] and the [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]]. ==See also== *{{slink|Law of Japan|Historical developments}} *[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] *[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] *[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] *[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] *[[:en:Station bell|Station bell]] *[[:en:Fengjian|Fengjian]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[:en:Kan'ichi Asakawa|Asakawa]], Kan'ichi. (1903). [https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_K1MuAAAAYAAJ ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: [[:en:Shueisha|Shueisha]]. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org] * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691] * [[:en:George Sansom|Sansom]], George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334.'' Stanford: [[:en:Stanford University Press|Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-0523-2}} * Haley, John Owen. ''Authority Without Power: Law and the Japanese Paradox'' (Oxford, 1994), {{ISBN|0-19-509257-0}} ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20150226180044/http://forums.samurai-archives.com/viewtopic.php?t=3397 A full listing of public posts in the Ritsuryō System can be found here] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Classical Japan]] [[Category:Legal history of Japan]] }} [[en:Ritsuryō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[de:Ritsuryō]] [[es:Ritsuryō]] [[fa:ریتسوریو]] [[fi:Ritsuryō]] [[fr:Ritsuryō]] [[hr:Ritsuryō]] [[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]] [[id:Ritsuryō]] [[it:Ritsuryō]] [[ja:律令制]] [[ko:일본의 율령제]] [[nl:Ritsuryo]] [[pt:Ritsuryō]] [[ru:Система рицурё]] [[sr:Рицурјо]] [[th:ริตสึเรียว]] [[tr:Ritsuryō]] [[uk:Система ріцурьо]] [[vi:Ritsuryō]] [[zh:日本律令制]] {{wikidata link|Q742460}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[az:Ritsuryo]][[cs:Ricurjó]][[de:Ritsuryō]][[en:Ritsuryō]][[es:Ritsuryō]][[fa:ریتسوریو]][[fi:Ritsuryō]][[fr:Ritsuryō]][[hr:Ritsuryō]][[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]][[id:Ritsuryō]][[it:Ritsuryō]][[ja:律令制]][[ko:일본의 율령제]][[nl:Ritsuryo]][[pt:Ritsuryō]][[ru:Система рицурё]][[sr:Рицурјо]][[th:ริตสึเรียว]][[tr:Ritsuryō]][[uk:Система ріцурьо]][[vi:Ritsuryō]][[zh:日本律令制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q742460]] Wikidata name: ritsuryō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cleanup lang|iso=ja|date=June 2024}}{{Short description|Historical law system in Japan}} {{Daijō-kan}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Ritsuryō'''''|律令||{{IPA|ja|ɾitsɯɾʲoː|lang}}}} is the historical [[:en:Japanese legal system|legal system]] based on the philosophies of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] and [[:en:Legalism (Chinese philosophy)|Chinese Legalism]] in [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]]. The political system in accord to Ritsuryō is called "Ritsuryō-sei" (律令制). ''Kyaku'' (格) are [[:en:amendment|amendment]]s of Ritsuryō, ''Shiki'' (式) are enactments. Ritsuryō defines both a {{nihongo|criminal code|律|Ritsu}} and an {{nihongo|administrative code|令|Ryō}}. During the late [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] (late 6th century – 710) and [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court in Kyoto]], trying to replicate [[:en:China|China]]'s rigorous political system from the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]], created and enforced some collections of Ritsuryō. Over the course of centuries, the ''ritsuryō'' state produced more and more information which was carefully archived; however, with the passage of time in the Heian period, ''ritsuryō'' institutions evolved into a political and cultural system without feedback.[http://202.231.40.34/jpub/pdf/jr/IJ1508.pdf Mesheryakov, Alexander. (2003). "On the Quantity of Written Data Produced by the Ritsuryō State"], ''Japan Review'', 15:187–199. In 645, the [[:en:Taika reforms|Taika reforms]] were the first signs of implementation of the system.Asakawa, Kan'ichi. (1903). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in the Reform of 645'', p. 324 n.3. Major re-statements of Ritsuryō included the following:Asakawa, p. 13. * ''[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi-ryō]]'' (近江令, 669) – 22 volumes of administrative code, of disputed existence * ''[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka-kiyomihara-ryō]]'' (飛鳥浄御原令, 689) – 22 volumes of administrative code * ''[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō-ritsuryō]]'' (大宝律令, 701) – of major influence, 11 volumes of administrative code, 6 volumes of criminal code * ''[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō-ritsuryō]]'' (養老律令, 720, enacted in 757) – 10 volumes of administrative code, 10 volumes of criminal code, revised edition of the ''Taihō-ritsuryō'' ==Main achievements== ===Government and administration=== In the later half of the seventh century, the {{nihongo|Kokugunri system|国郡里制|kokugunri-sei}} was introduced, dividing the [[:en:Gokishichidō|regions of Japan]] into several administrative divisions. * {{nihongo|[[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces]]|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|[[:en:Districts of Japan|Districts]]|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|composed of 50 homes}} In 715 CE, the {{nihongo|Gōri system|郷里制|gōri-sei}} was introduced, resulting in the following. * {{nihongo|Provinces|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|Districts|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 townships}} * {{nihongo|Townships|郷|gō|composed of 50 homes total, and further divided into two or three neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|usu. composed of approx. 10–25 homes}} This system was abandoned in 740 CE. ===Centralization of authority=== The ritsuryō system also established a central administrative government, with the emperor at its head. Two departments were set up: *The [[Department of Divinities]] (神祇官, Department of Worship), in charge of rituals and clergy *The [[Daijō-kan]] (太政官, Department of State), divided into eight ministries. Posts of those public Departments were all divided into four Ranks (''[[shitōkan|shitō]]''): ''kami'' (長官), ''suke'' (次官), ''jō'' (判官) and ''sakan'' (主典). This ubiquitous pattern would be replicated consistently, even amongst members of the court whose functions had little to do with those kinds of powers and responsibilities which are conventionally associated with governing – for example: ;Court musicians * {{nihongo|Chief court musician|雅楽頭,|''Uta no kami''}}.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 429.] * {{nihongo|First assistant court musician|雅楽助,|''Uta no suke''}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant court musician|雅楽允,|''Uta no jō''}}.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 p. 430.] * {{nihongo|Alternate assistant court musicians|雅楽属,|''Uta no sakan''}}. ;Court pharmacists * {{nihongo|Chief court pharmacist|典薬頭,|''Ten'yaku no kami''}}.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 p. 434.] * {{nihongo|First assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬助|,''Ten'yaku no suke''}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬允|,''Ten'yaku no jō''}}. * {{nihongo|Alternate assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬属|,''Ten'yaku no sakan''}}. === Establishment of court Rank === {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:80%; text-align:center; float:right" |- !colspan="2" |{{Nowrap|Rank}} !colspan="2" |Ikai |- !1 |rowspan="2"|一位
first |正一位 |shō ichi-i |- !2 |従一位 |ju ichi-i |- !3 |rowspan="2"|二位
second |正二位 |shō ni-i |- !4 |従二位 |ju ni-i |- !5 |rowspan="2"|三位
third |正三位 |shō san-mi |- !6 |従三位 |ju san-mi |- !7 |rowspan="4"|四位
4th |{{Nowrap|正四位上|1=Senior Fourth Rank|lt=Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade}} |shō shi-i no jō |- !8 |正四位下 |shō shi-i no ge |- !9 |従四位上 |ju shi-i no jō |- !10 |従四位下 |ju shi-i no ge |- !11 |rowspan="4"|五位
5th |正五位上 |shō go-i no jō |- !12 |正五位下 |shō go-i no ge |- !13 |従五位上 |ju go-i no jō |- !14 |従五位下 |ju go-i no ge |- !15 |rowspan="4"|六位
6th |正六位上 |shō roku-i no jō |- !16 |正六位下 |shō roku-i no ge |- !17 |従六位上 |ju roku-i no jō |- !18 |従六位下 |ju roku-i no ge |- !19 |rowspan="4"|七位
7th |正七位上 |shō shichi-i no jō |- !20 |正七位下 |shō shichi-i no ge |- !21 |従七位上 |ju shichi-i no jō |- !22 |従七位下 |ju shichi-i no ge |- !23 |rowspan="4"|八位
8th |正八位上 |shō hachi-i no jō |- !24 |正八位下 |shō hachi-i no ge |- !25 |従八位上 |ju hachi-i no jō |- !26 |従八位下 |ju hachi-i no ge |- !27 |rowspan="4"|初位The initial Ranks were subdivided into "greater" (大 ''dai'') and "lesser" (少 ''shō'') Ranks.
initial |大初位上 |dai so-i no jō |- !28 |大初位下 |dai so-i no ge |- !29 |少初位上 |shō so-i no jō |- !30 |少初位下 |shō so-i no ge |} A global system of Ranking for all public posts (官 ''kan'', 官職 ''[[kanshoku]]'') was introduced with over 30 Ranks (位 ''i'', 位階 ''[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles#Court Ranks|ikai]]''), regulating strictly which posts could be accessed by which Rank. Ranking was supposed to be mostly merit-based, the children of high-Ranking public officials were nonetheless granted a minimal Rank. [[Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in asia#Japan|This provision]] (蔭位の制 ''on'i no sei'') existed in the Tang law, however under the Japanese ritsuryo Ranks for which it was applied were higher as well as the Ranks obtained by the children. The highest Rank in the system was the first Rank (一位 ''ichi-i''), proceeding downwards to the eighth Rank (八位 ''hachi-i''), held by menials in the court. Below this, an initial Rank called ''so-i'' (初位) existed, but offered few rights.{{cite book | last = Borgen | first = Robert | title = Sugawara no Michizane and the Early Heian Court | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1994 | isbn = 0-8248-1590-4 | pages=13–14}} The top six Ranks were considered true aristocracy (貴 ''ki''), and were subdivided into "senior" (正 ''shō'') and "junior" (従 ''ju'') Ranks (e.g. senior third-Rank [正三位 ''shō san-mi''], junior second-Rank [従二位 ''ju ni-i'']). Below the third Rank, a further subdivision between "upper" (上 ''jō'') and "lower" (下 ''ge'') existed, allowing for Ranks such as “junior fourth Rank lower” (従四位下 ''ju shi-i no ge'') or “senior sixth Rank upper” (正六位上 ''shō roku-i no jō''). Promotion in Ranks was often a very gradual, bureaucratic process, and in the early days of the Codes, one could not advance beyond sixth Rank except by rare exception, thus causing a natural cut-off point between the aristocrats (fifth-Rank and above [貴族 ''kizoku'']) and the menials (sixth-Rank and below [地下 ''jige'']). Additionally, income in the form of ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' (石, 1 koku = about 150 kilograms), or bushels of rice from the provinces, increased dramatically as one advanced in Rank. The average sixth-Rank official might earn 22 koku of rice a year, but the fifth Rank might earn 225 koku of rice, while a third Rank official could earn as much as 6,957 a year. Registration of the citizens (戸籍 ''koseki''), updated every 6 years, and a yearly tax book (計帳 ''keichō'') were established. Based on the ''keichō'', a tax system was established called (租庸調 ''So-yō-chō''). Tax was levied on rice crops but also on several local products (e.g. cotton, salt, tissue) sent to the capital. The system also established local corvée at a provincial level by orders of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] (国司), a corvée at the Capital (although the corvée at the capital could be replaced by goods sent) and military service. ===Criminal code=== A criminal system was introduced, with {{nihongo|five levels of punishment|五刑|gokei}}. *{{nihongo|[[:en:Caning|Caning]]|笞|chi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 10, 20, 30, 40 or 50 strikes on the buttocks. *{{nihongo|Public caning|杖|jō}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 60, 70, 80, 90 or 100 strikes on the buttocks, performed in public, using a slightly thicker cane than was used for ''chi''. *{{nihongo|Imprisonment|徒|zu}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, imprisonment for 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5 or 3 years. *[[:en:Exile|Exile]] (流, ''ru'') Depending on the severity of the crime, {{nihongo|nearby exile|近流|''konru''}}, {{nihongo|semi-distant exile|中流|''chūru''}}, or {{nihongo|distant exile|遠流|''onru''}}. *{{nihongo|Death|死|shi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, death by {{nihongo|hanging|絞|kō}} or {{nihongo|decapitation|斬|zan}}. It defined {{nihongo|eight heavy crimes|八虐|hachigyaku}} that were exempt from amnesty. The code was based on the [[:en:Ten Abominations|Ten Abominations]] of the Tang code, but two crimes related to family life—family discord and disruption of the family (through incest, adultery, etc.) —were removed. ===Handen-Shūju=== In accordance with Chinese legal codes, land as well as citizens were to be "public property" (公地公民). One of the major pillars of the Ritsuryō was the introduction of the '''Handen-Shūju''' (班田収受制) system, similar to the [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]] in China. The Handen-Shūju regulated land ownership. Based on the registration, each citizen over 6 was entitled to a {{nihongo|"distributed field"|口分田|kubunden}}, subject to taxation (approx. 3% of crops). The area of each field was 2 {{nihongo|''tan''|段}} for men (approx. 22 ares total), and two-thirds of this amount for women. (However, the [[:en:Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō|Shinuhi and Kenin castes]] were only entitled to 1/3 of this area). The field was returned to the country at death. Land belonging to shrines and temples was exempt from taxation. Collection and redistribution of land took place every 6 years. ===Castes=== {{main|Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō}} The population was divided in two castes, ''Ryōmin'' (良民) (furthermore divided into 4 sub-castes{{Citation needed|date=December 2007}}) and ''Senmin'' (賤民) (divided into 5 sub-castes), the latter being close to slaves. Citizens wore different colors according to their caste. ==Evolution of Ritsuryō application== Several modifications were added over time. In order to promote cultivation, a law allowing the ownership for three generations of newly arable fields was promulgated in 723 (三世一身の法, ''Sanze-isshin Law'') and then without limits in 743 (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Law''). This led to the appearance of large private lands, the first ''[[shōen]]s''. Strict application of the Handen-Shūju system decayed in the 8th and 9th century.{{Cite book|title=A history of Japan|last=D.|first=Totman, Conrad|date=2000-01-01|publisher=Blackwell Publishers|isbn=1557860769|pages=100|oclc=41967280}} In an attempt to maintain the system, the period between each collection/distribution was extended to 12 years under [[Emperor Kanmu]]. At the beginning of [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the system was almost not enforced. The last collection/distribution took place between 902 and 903. The caste system was less and less strictly enforced. Some Ryōmin would wed Senmin to avoid taxation, and Senmin/Ryōmin children would become Ryōmin. At the end of the 9th century / beginning of the 10th, the caste system was practically void of its substance. Hereditary high-Ranks for public posts led to the monopoly of occupation of the most important posts by a limited number of families, in effect a nobility, amongst which the [[Fujiwara clan]], [[Minamoto clan]], [[Taira clan]] and the [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]]. ==See also== *{{slink|Law of Japan|Historical developments}} *[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] *[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] *[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] *[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] *[[:en:Station bell|Station bell]] *[[:en:Fengjian|Fengjian]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[:en:Kan'ichi Asakawa|Asakawa]], Kan'ichi. ''Authority Without Power: Law and the Japanese Paradox'' (Oxford, 1994), {{ISBN|0-19-509257-0}} ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20150226180044/http://forums.samurai-archives.com/viewtopic.php?t=3397 A full listing of public posts in the Ritsuryō System can be found here] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Classical Japan]] [[Category:Legal history of Japan]] }} [[en:Ritsuryō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[de:Ritsuryō]] [[es:Ritsuryō]] [[fa:ریتسوریو]] [[fi:Ritsuryō]] [[fr:Ritsuryō]] [[hr:Ritsuryō]] [[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]] [[id:Ritsuryō]] [[it:Ritsuryō]] [[ja:律令制]] [[ko:일본의 율령제]] [[nl:Ritsuryo]] [[pt:Ritsuryō]] [[ru:Система рицурё]] [[sr:Рицурјо]] [[th:ริตสึเรียว]] [[tr:Ritsuryō]] [[uk:Система ріцурьо]] [[vi:Ritsuryō]] [[zh:日本律令制]] {{wikidata link|Q742460}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

(2003). "On the Quantity of Written Data Produced by the Ritsuryō State"], ''Japan Review'', 15:187–199. In 645, the [[:en:Taika reforms|Taika reforms]] were the first signs of implementation of the system.Asakawa, Kan'ichi. (1903). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in the Reform of 645'', p. 324 n.3. Major re-statements of Ritsuryō included the following:Asakawa, p. 13. * ''[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi-ryō]]'' (近江令, 669) – 22 volumes of administrative code, of disputed existence * ''[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka-kiyomihara-ryō]]'' (飛鳥浄御原令, 689) – 22 volumes of administrative code * ''[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō-ritsuryō]]'' (大宝律令, 701) – of major influence, 11 volumes of administrative code, 6 volumes of criminal code * ''[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō-ritsuryō]]'' (養老律令, 720, enacted in 757) – 10 volumes of administrative code, 10 volumes of criminal code, revised edition of the ''Taihō-ritsuryō'' ==Main achievements== ===Government and administration=== In the later half of the seventh century, the {{nihongo|Kokugunri system|国郡里制|kokugunri-sei}} was introduced, dividing the [[:en:Gokishichidō|regions of Japan]] into several administrative divisions. * {{nihongo|[[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces]]|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|[[:en:Districts of Japan|Districts]]|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|composed of 50 homes}} In 715 CE, the {{nihongo|Gōri system|郷里制|gōri-sei}} was introduced, resulting in the following. * {{nihongo|Provinces|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|Districts|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 townships}} * {{nihongo|Townships|郷|gō|composed of 50 homes total, and further divided into two or three neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|usu. composed of approx. 10–25 homes}} This system was abandoned in 740 CE. ===Centralization of authority=== The ritsuryō system also established a central administrative government, with the emperor at its head. Two departments were set up: *The [[Department of Divinities]] (神祇官, Department of Worship), in charge of rituals and clergy *The [[Daijō-kan]] (太政官, Department of State), divided into eight ministries. Posts of those public Departments were all divided into four Ranks (''[[shitōkan|shitō]]''): ''kami'' (長官), ''suke'' (次官), ''jō'' (判官) and ''sakan'' (主典). This ubiquitous pattern would be replicated consistently, even amongst members of the court whose functions had little to do with those kinds of powers and responsibilities which are conventionally associated with governing – for example: ;Court musicians * {{nihongo|Chief court musician|雅楽頭,|''Uta no kami''}}.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 429.] * {{nihongo|First assistant court musician|雅楽助,|''Uta no suke''}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant court musician|雅楽允,|''Uta no jō''}}.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 p. 430.] * {{nihongo|Alternate assistant court musicians|雅楽属,|''Uta no sakan''}}. ;Court pharmacists * {{nihongo|Chief court pharmacist|典薬頭,|''Ten'yaku no kami''}}.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 p. 434.] * {{nihongo|First assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬助|,''Ten'yaku no suke''}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬允|,''Ten'yaku no jō''}}. * {{nihongo|Alternate assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬属|,''Ten'yaku no sakan''}}. === Establishment of court Rank === {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:80%; text-align:center; float:right" |- !colspan="2" |{{Nowrap|Rank}} !colspan="2" |Ikai |- !1 |rowspan="2"|一位
first |正一位 |shō ichi-i |- !2 |従一位 |ju ichi-i |- !3 |rowspan="2"|二位
second |正二位 |shō ni-i |- !4 |従二位 |ju ni-i |- !5 |rowspan="2"|三位
third |正三位 |shō san-mi |- !6 |従三位 |ju san-mi |- !7 |rowspan="4"|四位
4th |{{Nowrap|正四位上|1=Senior Fourth Rank|lt=Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade}} |shō shi-i no jō |- !8 |正四位下 |shō shi-i no ge |- !9 |従四位上 |ju shi-i no jō |- !10 |従四位下 |ju shi-i no ge |- !11 |rowspan="4"|五位
5th |正五位上 |shō go-i no jō |- !12 |正五位下 |shō go-i no ge |- !13 |従五位上 |ju go-i no jō |- !14 |従五位下 |ju go-i no ge |- !15 |rowspan="4"|六位
6th |正六位上 |shō roku-i no jō |- !16 |正六位下 |shō roku-i no ge |- !17 |従六位上 |ju roku-i no jō |- !18 |従六位下 |ju roku-i no ge |- !19 |rowspan="4"|七位
7th |正七位上 |shō shichi-i no jō |- !20 |正七位下 |shō shichi-i no ge |- !21 |従七位上 |ju shichi-i no jō |- !22 |従七位下 |ju shichi-i no ge |- !23 |rowspan="4"|八位
8th |正八位上 |shō hachi-i no jō |- !24 |正八位下 |shō hachi-i no ge |- !25 |従八位上 |ju hachi-i no jō |- !26 |従八位下 |ju hachi-i no ge |- !27 |rowspan="4"|初位The initial Ranks were subdivided into "greater" (大 ''dai'') and "lesser" (少 ''shō'') Ranks.
initial |大初位上 |dai so-i no jō |- !28 |大初位下 |dai so-i no ge |- !29 |少初位上 |shō so-i no jō |- !30 |少初位下 |shō so-i no ge |} A global system of Ranking for all public posts (官 ''kan'', 官職 ''[[kanshoku]]'') was introduced with over 30 Ranks (位 ''i'', 位階 ''[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles#Court Ranks|ikai]]''), regulating strictly which posts could be accessed by which Rank. Ranking was supposed to be mostly merit-based, the children of high-Ranking public officials were nonetheless granted a minimal Rank. [[Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in asia#Japan|This provision]] (蔭位の制 ''on'i no sei'') existed in the Tang law, however under the Japanese ritsuryo Ranks for which it was applied were higher as well as the Ranks obtained by the children. The highest Rank in the system was the first Rank (一位 ''ichi-i''), proceeding downwards to the eighth Rank (八位 ''hachi-i''), held by menials in the court. Below this, an initial Rank called ''so-i'' (初位) existed, but offered few rights.{{cite book | last = Borgen | first = Robert | title = Sugawara no Michizane and the Early Heian Court | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1994 | isbn = 0-8248-1590-4 | pages=13–14}} The top six Ranks were considered true aristocracy (貴 ''ki''), and were subdivided into "senior" (正 ''shō'') and "junior" (従 ''ju'') Ranks (e.g. senior third-Rank [正三位 ''shō san-mi''], junior second-Rank [従二位 ''ju ni-i'']). Below the third Rank, a further subdivision between "upper" (上 ''jō'') and "lower" (下 ''ge'') existed, allowing for Ranks such as “junior fourth Rank lower” (従四位下 ''ju shi-i no ge'') or “senior sixth Rank upper” (正六位上 ''shō roku-i no jō''). Promotion in Ranks was often a very gradual, bureaucratic process, and in the early days of the Codes, one could not advance beyond sixth Rank except by rare exception, thus causing a natural cut-off point between the aristocrats (fifth-Rank and above [貴族 ''kizoku'']) and the menials (sixth-Rank and below [地下 ''jige'']). Additionally, income in the form of ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' (石, 1 koku = about 150 kilograms), or bushels of rice from the provinces, increased dramatically as one advanced in Rank. The average sixth-Rank official might earn 22 koku of rice a year, but the fifth Rank might earn 225 koku of rice, while a third Rank official could earn as much as 6,957 a year. Registration of the citizens (戸籍 ''koseki''), updated every 6 years, and a yearly tax book (計帳 ''keichō'') were established. Based on the ''keichō'', a tax system was established called (租庸調 ''So-yō-chō''). Tax was levied on rice crops but also on several local products (e.g. cotton, salt, tissue) sent to the capital. The system also established local corvée at a provincial level by orders of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] (国司), a corvée at the Capital (although the corvée at the capital could be replaced by goods sent) and military service. ===Criminal code=== A criminal system was introduced, with {{nihongo|five levels of punishment|五刑|gokei}}. *{{nihongo|[[:en:Caning|Caning]]|笞|chi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 10, 20, 30, 40 or 50 strikes on the buttocks. *{{nihongo|Public caning|杖|jō}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 60, 70, 80, 90 or 100 strikes on the buttocks, performed in public, using a slightly thicker cane than was used for ''chi''. *{{nihongo|Imprisonment|徒|zu}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, imprisonment for 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5 or 3 years. *[[:en:Exile|Exile]] (流, ''ru'') Depending on the severity of the crime, {{nihongo|nearby exile|近流|''konru''}}, {{nihongo|semi-distant exile|中流|''chūru''}}, or {{nihongo|distant exile|遠流|''onru''}}. *{{nihongo|Death|死|shi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, death by {{nihongo|hanging|絞|kō}} or {{nihongo|decapitation|斬|zan}}. It defined {{nihongo|eight heavy crimes|八虐|hachigyaku}} that were exempt from amnesty. The code was based on the [[:en:Ten Abominations|Ten Abominations]] of the Tang code, but two crimes related to family life—family discord and disruption of the family (through incest, adultery, etc.) —were removed. ===Handen-Shūju=== In accordance with Chinese legal codes, land as well as citizens were to be "public property" (公地公民). One of the major pillars of the Ritsuryō was the introduction of the '''Handen-Shūju''' (班田収受制) system, similar to the [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]] in China. The Handen-Shūju regulated land ownership. Based on the registration, each citizen over 6 was entitled to a {{nihongo|"distributed field"|口分田|kubunden}}, subject to taxation (approx. 3% of crops). The area of each field was 2 {{nihongo|''tan''|段}} for men (approx. 22 ares total), and two-thirds of this amount for women. (However, the [[:en:Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō|Shinuhi and Kenin castes]] were only entitled to 1/3 of this area). The field was returned to the country at death. Land belonging to shrines and temples was exempt from taxation. Collection and redistribution of land took place every 6 years. ===Castes=== {{main|Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō}} The population was divided in two castes, ''Ryōmin'' (良民) (furthermore divided into 4 sub-castes{{Citation needed|date=December 2007}}) and ''Senmin'' (賤民) (divided into 5 sub-castes), the latter being close to slaves. Citizens wore different colors according to their caste. ==Evolution of Ritsuryō application== Several modifications were added over time. In order to promote cultivation, a law allowing the ownership for three generations of newly arable fields was promulgated in 723 (三世一身の法, ''Sanze-isshin Law'') and then without limits in 743 (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Law''). This led to the appearance of large private lands, the first ''[[shōen]]s''. Strict application of the Handen-Shūju system decayed in the 8th and 9th century.{{Cite book|title=A history of Japan|last=D.|first=Totman, Conrad|date=2000-01-01|publisher=Blackwell Publishers|isbn=1557860769|pages=100|oclc=41967280}} In an attempt to maintain the system, the period between each collection/distribution was extended to 12 years under [[Emperor Kanmu]]. At the beginning of [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the system was almost not enforced. The last collection/distribution took place between 902 and 903. The caste system was less and less strictly enforced. Some Ryōmin would wed Senmin to avoid taxation, and Senmin/Ryōmin children would become Ryōmin. At the end of the 9th century / beginning of the 10th, the caste system was practically void of its substance. Hereditary high-Ranks for public posts led to the monopoly of occupation of the most important posts by a limited number of families, in effect a nobility, amongst which the [[Fujiwara clan]], [[Minamoto clan]], [[Taira clan]] and the [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]]. ==See also== *{{slink|Law of Japan|Historical developments}} *[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] *[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] *[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] *[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] *[[:en:Station bell|Station bell]] *[[:en:Fengjian|Fengjian]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[:en:Kan'ichi Asakawa|Asakawa]], Kan'ichi. [[az:Ritsuryo]][[cs:Ricurjó]][[de:Ritsuryō]][[en:Ritsuryō]][[es:Ritsuryō]][[fa:ریتسوریو]][[fi:Ritsuryō]][[fr:Ritsuryō]][[hr:Ritsuryō]][[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]][[id:Ritsuryō]][[it:Ritsuryō]][[ja:律令制]][[ko:일본의 율령제]][[nl:Ritsuryo]][[pt:Ritsuryō]][[ru:Система рицурё]][[sr:Рицурјо]][[th:ริตสึเรียว]][[tr:Ritsuryō]][[uk:Система ріцурьо]][[vi:Ritsuryō]][[zh:日本律令制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q742460]] Wikidata name: ritsuryō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cleanup lang|iso=ja|date=June 2024}}{{Short description|Historical law system in Japan}} {{Daijō-kan}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Ritsuryō'''''|律令||{{IPA|ja|ɾitsɯɾʲoː|lang}}}} is the historical [[:en:Japanese legal system|legal system]] based on the philosophies of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] and [[:en:Legalism (Chinese philosophy)|Chinese Legalism]] in [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]]. The political system in accord to Ritsuryō is called "Ritsuryō-sei" (律令制). ''Kyaku'' (格) are [[:en:amendment|amendment]]s of Ritsuryō, ''Shiki'' (式) are enactments. Ritsuryō defines both a {{nihongo|criminal code|律|Ritsu}} and an {{nihongo|administrative code|令|Ryō}}. During the late [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] (late 6th century – 710) and [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court in Kyoto]], trying to replicate [[:en:China|China]]'s rigorous political system from the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]], created and enforced some collections of Ritsuryō. Over the course of centuries, the ''ritsuryō'' state produced more and more information which was carefully archived; however, with the passage of time in the Heian period, ''ritsuryō'' institutions evolved into a political and cultural system without feedback.[http://202.231.40.34/jpub/pdf/jr/IJ1508.pdf Mesheryakov, Alexander. (2003). "On the Quantity of Written Data Produced by the Ritsuryō State"], ''Japan Review'', 15:187–199. In 645, the [[:en:Taika reforms|Taika reforms]] were the first signs of implementation of the system.Asakawa, Kan'ichi. (1903). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in the Reform of 645'', p. 324 n.3. Major re-statements of Ritsuryō included the following:Asakawa, p. 13. * ''[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi-ryō]]'' (近江令, 669) – 22 volumes of administrative code, of disputed existence * ''[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka-kiyomihara-ryō]]'' (飛鳥浄御原令, 689) – 22 volumes of administrative code * ''[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō-ritsuryō]]'' (大宝律令, 701) – of major influence, 11 volumes of administrative code, 6 volumes of criminal code * ''[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō-ritsuryō]]'' (養老律令, 720, enacted in 757) – 10 volumes of administrative code, 10 volumes of criminal code, revised edition of the ''Taihō-ritsuryō'' ==Main achievements== ===Government and administration=== In the later half of the seventh century, the {{nihongo|Kokugunri system|国郡里制|kokugunri-sei}} was introduced, dividing the [[:en:Gokishichidō|regions of Japan]] into several administrative divisions. * {{nihongo|[[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces]]|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|[[:en:Districts of Japan|Districts]]|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|composed of 50 homes}} In 715 CE, the {{nihongo|Gōri system|郷里制|gōri-sei}} was introduced, resulting in the following. * {{nihongo|Provinces|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|Districts|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 townships}} * {{nihongo|Townships|郷|gō|composed of 50 homes total, and further divided into two or three neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|usu. composed of approx. 10–25 homes}} This system was abandoned in 740 CE. ===Centralization of authority=== The ritsuryō system also established a central administrative government, with the emperor at its head. Two departments were set up: *The [[Department of Divinities]] (神祇官, Department of Worship), in charge of rituals and clergy *The [[Daijō-kan]] (太政官, Department of State), divided into eight ministries. Posts of those public Departments were all divided into four Ranks (''[[shitōkan|shitō]]''): ''kami'' (長官), ''suke'' (次官), ''jō'' (判官) and ''sakan'' (主典). Below this, an initial Rank called ''so-i'' (初位) existed, but offered few rights.{{cite book | last = Borgen | first = Robert | title = Sugawara no Michizane and the Early Heian Court | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1994 | isbn = 0-8248-1590-4 | pages=13–14}} The top six Ranks were considered true aristocracy (貴 ''ki''), and were subdivided into "senior" (正 ''shō'') and "junior" (従 ''ju'') Ranks (e.g. senior third-Rank [正三位 ''shō san-mi''], junior second-Rank [従二位 ''ju ni-i'']). Below the third Rank, a further subdivision between "upper" (上 ''jō'') and "lower" (下 ''ge'') existed, allowing for Ranks such as “junior fourth Rank lower” (従四位下 ''ju shi-i no ge'') or “senior sixth Rank upper” (正六位上 ''shō roku-i no jō''). Promotion in Ranks was often a very gradual, bureaucratic process, and in the early days of the Codes, one could not advance beyond sixth Rank except by rare exception, thus causing a natural cut-off point between the aristocrats (fifth-Rank and above [貴族 ''kizoku'']) and the menials (sixth-Rank and below [地下 ''jige'']). Additionally, income in the form of ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' (石, 1 koku = about 150 kilograms), or bushels of rice from the provinces, increased dramatically as one advanced in Rank. The average sixth-Rank official might earn 22 koku of rice a year, but the fifth Rank might earn 225 koku of rice, while a third Rank official could earn as much as 6,957 a year. Registration of the citizens (戸籍 ''koseki''), updated every 6 years, and a yearly tax book (計帳 ''keichō'') were established. Based on the ''keichō'', a tax system was established called (租庸調 ''So-yō-chō''). Tax was levied on rice crops but also on several local products (e.g. cotton, salt, tissue) sent to the capital. The system also established local corvée at a provincial level by orders of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] (国司), a corvée at the Capital (although the corvée at the capital could be replaced by goods sent) and military service. ===Criminal code=== A criminal system was introduced, with {{nihongo|five levels of punishment|五刑|gokei}}. *{{nihongo|[[:en:Caning|Caning]]|笞|chi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 10, 20, 30, 40 or 50 strikes on the buttocks. *{{nihongo|Public caning|杖|jō}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 60, 70, 80, 90 or 100 strikes on the buttocks, performed in public, using a slightly thicker cane than was used for ''chi''. *{{nihongo|Imprisonment|徒|zu}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, imprisonment for 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5 or 3 years. *[[:en:Exile|Exile]] (流, ''ru'') Depending on the severity of the crime, {{nihongo|nearby exile|近流|''konru''}}, {{nihongo|semi-distant exile|中流|''chūru''}}, or {{nihongo|distant exile|遠流|''onru''}}. *{{nihongo|Death|死|shi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, death by {{nihongo|hanging|絞|kō}} or {{nihongo|decapitation|斬|zan}}. It defined {{nihongo|eight heavy crimes|八虐|hachigyaku}} that were exempt from amnesty. The code was based on the [[:en:Ten Abominations|Ten Abominations]] of the Tang code, but two crimes related to family life—family discord and disruption of the family (through incest, adultery, etc.) —were removed. ===Handen-Shūju=== In accordance with Chinese legal codes, land as well as citizens were to be "public property" (公地公民). One of the major pillars of the Ritsuryō was the introduction of the '''Handen-Shūju''' (班田収受制) system, similar to the [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]] in China. The Handen-Shūju regulated land ownership. Based on the registration, each citizen over 6 was entitled to a {{nihongo|"distributed field"|口分田|kubunden}}, subject to taxation (approx. 3% of crops). The area of each field was 2 {{nihongo|''tan''|段}} for men (approx. 22 ares total), and two-thirds of this amount for women. (However, the [[:en:Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō|Shinuhi and Kenin castes]] were only entitled to 1/3 of this area). The field was returned to the country at death. Land belonging to shrines and temples was exempt from taxation. Collection and redistribution of land took place every 6 years. ===Castes=== {{main|Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō}} The population was divided in two castes, ''Ryōmin'' (良民) (furthermore divided into 4 sub-castes{{Citation needed|date=December 2007}}) and ''Senmin'' (賤民) (divided into 5 sub-castes), the latter being close to slaves. Citizens wore different colors according to their caste. ==Evolution of Ritsuryō application== Several modifications were added over time. In order to promote cultivation, a law allowing the ownership for three generations of newly arable fields was promulgated in 723 (三世一身の法, ''Sanze-isshin Law'') and then without limits in 743 (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Law''). This led to the appearance of large private lands, the first ''[[shōen]]s''. Strict application of the Handen-Shūju system decayed in the 8th and 9th century.{{Cite book|title=A history of Japan|last=D.|first=Totman, Conrad|date=2000-01-01|publisher=Blackwell Publishers|isbn=1557860769|pages=100|oclc=41967280}} In an attempt to maintain the system, the period between each collection/distribution was extended to 12 years under [[Emperor Kanmu]]. At the beginning of [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the system was almost not enforced. The last collection/distribution took place between 902 and 903. The caste system was less and less strictly enforced. Some Ryōmin would wed Senmin to avoid taxation, and Senmin/Ryōmin children would become Ryōmin. At the end of the 9th century / beginning of the 10th, the caste system was practically void of its substance. Hereditary high-Ranks for public posts led to the monopoly of occupation of the most important posts by a limited number of families, in effect a nobility, amongst which the [[Fujiwara clan]], [[Minamoto clan]], [[Taira clan]] and the [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]]. ==See also== *{{slink|Law of Japan|Historical developments}} *[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] *[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] *[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] *[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] *[[:en:Station bell|Station bell]] *[[:en:Fengjian|Fengjian]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[:en:Kan'ichi Asakawa|Asakawa]], Kan'ichi. (1903). [https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_K1MuAAAAYAAJ ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: [[:en:Shueisha|Shueisha]]. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org] * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691] * [[:en:George Sansom|Sansom]], George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334.'' Stanford: [[:en:Stanford University Press|Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-0523-2}} * Haley, John Owen. ''Authority Without Power: Law and the Japanese Paradox'' (Oxford, 1994), {{ISBN|0-19-509257-0}} ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20150226180044/http://forums.samurai-archives.com/viewtopic.php?t=3397 A full listing of public posts in the Ritsuryō System can be found here] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Classical Japan]] [[Category:Legal history of Japan]] }} [[en:Ritsuryō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[de:Ritsuryō]] [[es:Ritsuryō]] [[fa:ریتسوریو]] [[fi:Ritsuryō]] [[fr:Ritsuryō]] [[hr:Ritsuryō]] [[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]] [[id:Ritsuryō]] [[it:Ritsuryō]] [[ja:律令制]] [[ko:일본의 율령제]] [[nl:Ritsuryo]] [[pt:Ritsuryō]] [[ru:Система рицурё]] [[sr:Рицурјо]] [[th:ริตสึเรียว]] [[tr:Ritsuryō]] [[uk:Система ріцурьо]] [[vi:Ritsuryō]] [[zh:日本律令制]] {{wikidata link|Q742460}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]][[Category:LINKED FROM WIKIDATA DO NOT OVERWRITE]]

[[az:Ritsuryo]][[cs:Ricurjó]][[de:Ritsuryō]][[en:Ritsuryō]][[es:Ritsuryō]][[fa:ریتسوریو]][[fi:Ritsuryō]][[fr:Ritsuryō]][[hr:Ritsuryō]][[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]][[id:Ritsuryō]][[it:Ritsuryō]][[ja:律令制]][[ko:일본의 율령제]][[nl:Ritsuryo]][[pt:Ritsuryō]][[ru:Система рицурё]][[sr:Рицурјо]][[th:ริตสึเรียว]][[tr:Ritsuryō]][[uk:Система ріцурьо]][[vi:Ritsuryō]][[zh:日本律令制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q742460]] Wikidata name: ritsuryō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cleanup lang|iso=ja|date=June 2024}}{{Short description|Historical law system in Japan}} {{Daijō-kan}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Ritsuryō'''''|律令||{{IPA|ja|ɾitsɯɾʲoː|lang}}}} is the historical [[:en:Japanese legal system|legal system]] based on the philosophies of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] and [[:en:Legalism (Chinese philosophy)|Chinese Legalism]] in [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]]. The political system in accord to Ritsuryō is called "Ritsuryō-sei" (律令制). ''Kyaku'' (格) are [[:en:amendment|amendment]]s of Ritsuryō, ''Shiki'' (式) are enactments. Ritsuryō defines both a {{nihongo|criminal code|律|Ritsu}} and an {{nihongo|administrative code|令|Ryō}}. During the late [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] (late 6th century – 710) and [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court in Kyoto]], trying to replicate [[:en:China|China]]'s rigorous political system from the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]], created and enforced some collections of Ritsuryō. Over the course of centuries, the ''ritsuryō'' state produced more and more information which was carefully archived; however, with the passage of time in the Heian period, ''ritsuryō'' institutions evolved into a political and cultural system without feedback.[http://202.231.40.34/jpub/pdf/jr/IJ1508.pdf Mesheryakov, Alexander. (2003). "On the Quantity of Written Data Produced by the Ritsuryō State"], ''Japan Review'', 15:187–199. In 645, the [[:en:Taika reforms|Taika reforms]] were the first signs of implementation of the system.Asakawa, Kan'ichi. (1903). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in the Reform of 645'', p. 324 n.3. Major re-statements of Ritsuryō included the following:Asakawa, p. 13. * ''[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi-ryō]]'' (近江令, 669) – 22 volumes of administrative code, of disputed existence * ''[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka-kiyomihara-ryō]]'' (飛鳥浄御原令, 689) – 22 volumes of administrative code * ''[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō-ritsuryō]]'' (大宝律令, 701) – of major influence, 11 volumes of administrative code, 6 volumes of criminal code * ''[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō-ritsuryō]]'' (養老律令, 720, enacted in 757) – 10 volumes of administrative code, 10 volumes of criminal code, revised edition of the ''Taihō-ritsuryō'' ==Main achievements== ===Government and administration=== In the later half of the seventh century, the {{nihongo|Kokugunri system|国郡里制|kokugunri-sei}} was introduced, dividing the [[:en:Gokishichidō|regions of Japan]] into several administrative divisions. * {{nihongo|[[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces]]|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|[[:en:Districts of Japan|Districts]]|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|composed of 50 homes}} In 715 CE, the {{nihongo|Gōri system|郷里制|gōri-sei}} was introduced, resulting in the following. * {{nihongo|Provinces|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|Districts|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 townships}} * {{nihongo|Townships|郷|gō|composed of 50 homes total, and further divided into two or three neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|usu. composed of approx. 10–25 homes}} This system was abandoned in 740 CE. ===Centralization of authority=== The ritsuryō system also established a central administrative government, with the emperor at its head. Two departments were set up: *The [[Department of Divinities]] (神祇官, Department of Worship), in charge of rituals and clergy *The [[Daijō-kan]] (太政官, Department of State), divided into eight ministries. Posts of those public Departments were all divided into four Ranks (''[[shitōkan|shitō]]''): ''kami'' (長官), ''suke'' (次官), ''jō'' (判官) and ''sakan'' (主典). This ubiquitous pattern would be replicated consistently, even amongst members of the court whose functions had little to do with those kinds of powers and responsibilities which are conventionally associated with governing – for example: ;Court musicians * {{nihongo|Chief court musician|雅楽頭,|''Uta no kami''}}.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 429.] * {{nihongo|First assistant court musician|雅楽助,|''Uta no suke''}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant court musician|雅楽允,|''Uta no jō''}}.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 p. 430.] * {{nihongo|Alternate assistant court musicians|雅楽属,|''Uta no sakan''}}. ;Court pharmacists * {{nihongo|Chief court pharmacist|典薬頭,|''Ten'yaku no kami''}}.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 p. 434.] * {{nihongo|First assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬助|,''Ten'yaku no suke''}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬允|,''Ten'yaku no jō''}}. * {{nihongo|Alternate assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬属|,''Ten'yaku no sakan''}}. === Establishment of court Rank === {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:80%; text-align:center; float:right" |- !colspan="2" |{{Nowrap|Rank}} !colspan="2" |Ikai |- !1 |rowspan="2"|一位
first |正一位 |shō ichi-i |- !2 |従一位 |ju ichi-i |- !3 |rowspan="2"|二位
second |正二位 |shō ni-i |- !4 |従二位 |ju ni-i |- !5 |rowspan="2"|三位
third |正三位 |shō san-mi |- !6 |従三位 |ju san-mi |- !7 |rowspan="4"|四位
4th |{{Nowrap|正四位上|1=Senior Fourth Rank|lt=Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade}} |shō shi-i no jō |- !8 |正四位下 |shō shi-i no ge |- !9 |従四位上 |ju shi-i no jō |- !10 |従四位下 |ju shi-i no ge |- !11 |rowspan="4"|五位
5th |正五位上 |shō go-i no jō |- !12 |正五位下 |shō go-i no ge |- !13 |従五位上 |ju go-i no jō |- !14 |従五位下 |ju go-i no ge |- !15 |rowspan="4"|六位
6th |正六位上 |shō roku-i no jō |- !16 |正六位下 |shō roku-i no ge |- !17 |従六位上 |ju roku-i no jō |- !18 |従六位下 |ju roku-i no ge |- !19 |rowspan="4"|七位
7th |正七位上 |shō shichi-i no jō |- !20 |正七位下 |shō shichi-i no ge |- !21 |従七位上 |ju shichi-i no jō |- !22 |従七位下 |ju shichi-i no ge |- !23 |rowspan="4"|八位
8th |正八位上 |shō hachi-i no jō |- !24 |正八位下 |shō hachi-i no ge |- !25 |従八位上 |ju hachi-i no jō |- !26 |従八位下 |ju hachi-i no ge |- !27 |rowspan="4"|初位The initial Ranks were subdivided into "greater" (大 ''dai'') and "lesser" (少 ''shō'') Ranks.
initial |大初位上 |dai so-i no jō |- !28 |大初位下 |dai so-i no ge |- !29 |少初位上 |shō so-i no jō |- !30 |少初位下 |shō so-i no ge |} A global system of Ranking for all public posts (官 ''kan'', 官職 ''[[kanshoku]]'') was introduced with over 30 Ranks (位 ''i'', 位階 ''[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles#Court Ranks|ikai]]''), regulating strictly which posts could be accessed by which Rank. Ranking was supposed to be mostly merit-based, the children of high-Ranking public officials were nonetheless granted a minimal Rank. [[Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in asia#Japan|This provision]] (蔭位の制 ''on'i no sei'') existed in the Tang law, however under the Japanese ritsuryo Ranks for which it was applied were higher as well as the Ranks obtained by the children. The highest Rank in the system was the first Rank (一位 ''ichi-i''), proceeding downwards to the eighth Rank (八位 ''hachi-i''), held by menials in the court. Below this, an initial Rank called ''so-i'' (初位) existed, but offered few rights.{{cite book | last = Borgen | first = Robert | title = Sugawara no Michizane and the Early Heian Court | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1994 | isbn = 0-8248-1590-4 | pages=13–14}} The top six Ranks were considered true aristocracy (貴 ''ki''), and were subdivided into "senior" (正 ''shō'') and "junior" (従 ''ju'') Ranks (e.g. senior third-Rank [正三位 ''shō san-mi''], junior second-Rank [従二位 ''ju ni-i'']). Below the third Rank, a further subdivision between "upper" (上 ''jō'') and "lower" (下 ''ge'') existed, allowing for Ranks such as “junior fourth Rank lower” (従四位下 ''ju shi-i no ge'') or “senior sixth Rank upper” (正六位上 ''shō roku-i no jō''). Promotion in Ranks was often a very gradual, bureaucratic process, and in the early days of the Codes, one could not advance beyond sixth Rank except by rare exception, thus causing a natural cut-off point between the aristocrats (fifth-Rank and above [貴族 ''kizoku'']) and the menials (sixth-Rank and below [地下 ''jige'']). Additionally, income in the form of ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' (石, 1 koku = about 150 kilograms), or bushels of rice from the provinces, increased dramatically as one advanced in Rank. The average sixth-Rank official might earn 22 koku of rice a year, but the fifth Rank might earn 225 koku of rice, while a third Rank official could earn as much as 6,957 a year. Registration of the citizens (戸籍 ''koseki''), updated every 6 years, and a yearly tax book (計帳 ''keichō'') were established. Based on the ''keichō'', a tax system was established called (租庸調 ''So-yō-chō''). Tax was levied on rice crops but also on several local products (e.g. cotton, salt, tissue) sent to the capital. The system also established local corvée at a provincial level by orders of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] (国司), a corvée at the Capital (although the corvée at the capital could be replaced by goods sent) and military service. ===Criminal code=== A criminal system was introduced, with {{nihongo|five levels of punishment|五刑|gokei}}. *{{nihongo|[[:en:Caning|Caning]]|笞|chi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 10, 20, 30, 40 or 50 strikes on the buttocks. *{{nihongo|Public caning|杖|jō}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 60, 70, 80, 90 or 100 strikes on the buttocks, performed in public, using a slightly thicker cane than was used for ''chi''. *{{nihongo|Imprisonment|徒|zu}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, imprisonment for 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5 or 3 years. *[[:en:Exile|Exile]] (流, ''ru'') Depending on the severity of the crime, {{nihongo|nearby exile|近流|''konru''}}, {{nihongo|semi-distant exile|中流|''chūru''}}, or {{nihongo|distant exile|遠流|''onru''}}. *{{nihongo|Death|死|shi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, death by {{nihongo|hanging|絞|kō}} or {{nihongo|decapitation|斬|zan}}. It defined {{nihongo|eight heavy crimes|八虐|hachigyaku}} that were exempt from amnesty. The code was based on the [[:en:Ten Abominations|Ten Abominations]] of the Tang code, but two crimes related to family life—family discord and disruption of the family (through incest, adultery, etc.) —were removed. ===Handen-Shūju=== In accordance with Chinese legal codes, land as well as citizens were to be "public property" (公地公民). One of the major pillars of the Ritsuryō was the introduction of the '''Handen-Shūju''' (班田収受制) system, similar to the [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]] in China. The Handen-Shūju regulated land ownership. Based on the registration, each citizen over 6 was entitled to a {{nihongo|"distributed field"|口分田|kubunden}}, subject to taxation (approx. 3% of crops). The area of each field was 2 {{nihongo|''tan''|段}} for men (approx. 22 ares total), and two-thirds of this amount for women. (However, the [[:en:Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō|Shinuhi and Kenin castes]] were only entitled to 1/3 of this area). The field was returned to the country at death. Land belonging to shrines and temples was exempt from taxation. Collection and redistribution of land took place every 6 years. ===Castes=== {{main|Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō}} The population was divided in two castes, ''Ryōmin'' (良民) (furthermore divided into 4 sub-castes{{Citation needed|date=December 2007}}) and ''Senmin'' (賤民) (divided into 5 sub-castes), the latter being close to slaves. Citizens wore different colors according to their caste. ==Evolution of Ritsuryō application== Several modifications were added over time. In order to promote cultivation, a law allowing the ownership for three generations of newly arable fields was promulgated in 723 (三世一身の法, ''Sanze-isshin Law'') and then without limits in 743 (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Law''). This led to the appearance of large private lands, the first ''[[shōen]]s''. Strict application of the Handen-Shūju system decayed in the 8th and 9th century.{{Cite book|title=A history of Japan|last=D.|first=Totman, Conrad|date=2000-01-01|publisher=Blackwell Publishers|isbn=1557860769|pages=100|oclc=41967280}} In an attempt to maintain the system, the period between each collection/distribution was extended to 12 years under [[Emperor Kanmu]]. At the beginning of [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the system was almost not enforced. The last collection/distribution took place between 902 and 903. The caste system was less and less strictly enforced. Some Ryōmin would wed Senmin to avoid taxation, and Senmin/Ryōmin children would become Ryōmin. At the end of the 9th century / beginning of the 10th, the caste system was practically void of its substance. Hereditary high-Ranks for public posts led to the monopoly of occupation of the most important posts by a limited number of families, in effect a nobility, amongst which the [[Fujiwara clan]], [[Minamoto clan]], [[Taira clan]] and the [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]]. ==See also== *{{slink|Law of Japan|Historical developments}} *[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] *[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] *[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] *[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] *[[:en:Station bell|Station bell]] *[[:en:Fengjian|Fengjian]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[:en:Kan'ichi Asakawa|Asakawa]], Kan'ichi. (1903). [https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_K1MuAAAAYAAJ ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: [[:en:Shueisha|Shueisha]]. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org] * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691] * [[:en:George Sansom|Sansom]], George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334.'' Stanford: [[:en:Stanford University Press|Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-0523-2}} * Haley, John Owen. ''Authority Without Power: Law and the Japanese Paradox'' (Oxford, 1994), {{ISBN|0-19-509257-0}} ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20150226180044/http://forums.samurai-archives.com/viewtopic.php?t=3397 A full listing of public posts in the Ritsuryō System can be found here] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Classical Japan]] [[Category:Legal history of Japan]] }} [[en:Ritsuryō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[de:Ritsuryō]] [[es:Ritsuryō]] [[fa:ریتسوریو]] [[fi:Ritsuryō]] [[fr:Ritsuryō]] [[hr:Ritsuryō]] [[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]] [[id:Ritsuryō]] [[it:Ritsuryō]] [[ja:律令制]] [[ko:일본의 율령제]] [[nl:Ritsuryo]] [[pt:Ritsuryō]] [[ru:Система рицурё]] [[sr:Рицурјо]] [[th:ริตสึเรียว]] [[tr:Ritsuryō]] [[uk:Система ріцурьо]] [[vi:Ritsuryō]] [[zh:日本律令制]] {{wikidata link|Q742460}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

[[az:Ritsuryo]][[cs:Ricurjó]][[de:Ritsuryō]][[en:Ritsuryō]][[es:Ritsuryō]][[fa:ریتسوریو]][[fi:Ritsuryō]][[fr:Ritsuryō]][[hr:Ritsuryō]][[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]][[id:Ritsuryō]][[it:Ritsuryō]][[ja:律令制]][[ko:일본의 율령제]][[nl:Ritsuryo]][[pt:Ritsuryō]][[ru:Система рицурё]][[sr:Рицурјо]][[th:ริตสึเรียว]][[tr:Ritsuryō]][[uk:Система ріцурьо]][[vi:Ritsuryō]][[zh:日本律令制]] [[az:Ritsuryo]][[cs:Ricurjó]][[de:Ritsuryō]][[en:Ritsuryō]][[es:Ritsuryō]][[fa:ریتسوریو]][[fi:Ritsuryō]][[fr:Ritsuryō]][[hr:Ritsuryō]][[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]][[id:Ritsuryō]][[it:Ritsuryō]][[ja:律令制]][[ko:일본의 율령제]][[nl:Ritsuryo]][[pt:Ritsuryō]][[ru:Система рицурё]][[sr:Рицурјо]][[th:ริตสึเรียว]][[tr:Ritsuryō]][[uk:Система ріцурьо]][[vi:Ritsuryō]][[zh:日本律令制]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q742460]] Wikidata name: ritsuryō [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:57, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Cleanup lang|iso=ja|date=June 2024}}{{Short description|Historical law system in Japan}} {{Daijō-kan}} {{Italic title|reason=}} {{nihongo|'''''Ritsuryō'''''|律令||{{IPA|ja|ɾitsɯɾʲoː|lang}}}} is the historical [[:en:Japanese legal system|legal system]] based on the philosophies of [[:en:Confucianism|Confucianism]] and [[:en:Legalism (Chinese philosophy)|Chinese Legalism]] in [[:en:Feudal Japan|Feudal Japan]]. The political system in accord to Ritsuryō is called "Ritsuryō-sei" (律令制). ''Kyaku'' (格) are [[:en:amendment|amendment]]s of Ritsuryō, ''Shiki'' (式) are enactments. Ritsuryō defines both a {{nihongo|criminal code|律|Ritsu}} and an {{nihongo|administrative code|令|Ryō}}. During the late [[:en:Asuka period|Asuka period]] (late 6th century – 710) and [[:en:Nara period|Nara period]] (710–794), the [[:en:Imperial Court in Kyoto|Imperial Court in Kyoto]], trying to replicate [[:en:China|China]]'s rigorous political system from the [[:en:Tang dynasty|Tang dynasty]], created and enforced some collections of Ritsuryō. Over the course of centuries, the ''ritsuryō'' state produced more and more information which was carefully archived; however, with the passage of time in the Heian period, ''ritsuryō'' institutions evolved into a political and cultural system without feedback.[http://202.231.40.34/jpub/pdf/jr/IJ1508.pdf Mesheryakov, Alexander. (2003). "On the Quantity of Written Data Produced by the Ritsuryō State"], ''Japan Review'', 15:187–199. In 645, the [[:en:Taika reforms|Taika reforms]] were the first signs of implementation of the system.Asakawa, Kan'ichi. (1903). ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan: A Study in the Reform of 645'', p. 324 n.3. Major re-statements of Ritsuryō included the following:Asakawa, p. 13. * ''[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi-ryō]]'' (近江令, 669) – 22 volumes of administrative code, of disputed existence * ''[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka-kiyomihara-ryō]]'' (飛鳥浄御原令, 689) – 22 volumes of administrative code * ''[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō-ritsuryō]]'' (大宝律令, 701) – of major influence, 11 volumes of administrative code, 6 volumes of criminal code * ''[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō-ritsuryō]]'' (養老律令, 720, enacted in 757) – 10 volumes of administrative code, 10 volumes of criminal code, revised edition of the ''Taihō-ritsuryō'' ==Main achievements== ===Government and administration=== In the later half of the seventh century, the {{nihongo|Kokugunri system|国郡里制|kokugunri-sei}} was introduced, dividing the [[:en:Gokishichidō|regions of Japan]] into several administrative divisions. * {{nihongo|[[:en:Provinces of Japan|Provinces]]|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|[[:en:Districts of Japan|Districts]]|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|composed of 50 homes}} In 715 CE, the {{nihongo|Gōri system|郷里制|gōri-sei}} was introduced, resulting in the following. * {{nihongo|Provinces|国|kuni|composed of numerous districts}} * {{nihongo|Districts|郡|gun, kōri|composed of 2–20 townships}} * {{nihongo|Townships|郷|gō|composed of 50 homes total, and further divided into two or three neighbourhoods}} * {{nihongo|Neighbourhoods|里|ri, sato|usu. composed of approx. 10–25 homes}} This system was abandoned in 740 CE. ===Centralization of authority=== The ritsuryō system also established a central administrative government, with the emperor at its head. Two departments were set up: *The [[Department of Divinities]] (神祇官, Department of Worship), in charge of rituals and clergy *The [[Daijō-kan]] (太政官, Department of State), divided into eight ministries. Posts of those public Departments were all divided into four Ranks (''[[shitōkan|shitō]]''): ''kami'' (長官), ''suke'' (次官), ''jō'' (判官) and ''sakan'' (主典). This ubiquitous pattern would be replicated consistently, even amongst members of the court whose functions had little to do with those kinds of powers and responsibilities which are conventionally associated with governing – for example: ;Court musicians * {{nihongo|Chief court musician|雅楽頭,|''Uta no kami''}}.Titsingh, Isaac. (1834). [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 ''Annales des empereurs du japon,'' p. 429.] * {{nihongo|First assistant court musician|雅楽助,|''Uta no suke''}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant court musician|雅楽允,|''Uta no jō''}}.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 p. 430.] * {{nihongo|Alternate assistant court musicians|雅楽属,|''Uta no sakan''}}. ;Court pharmacists * {{nihongo|Chief court pharmacist|典薬頭,|''Ten'yaku no kami''}}.Titsingh, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&pg=PP9&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran#PRA1-PA429,M1 p. 434.] * {{nihongo|First assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬助|,''Ten'yaku no suke''}}. * {{nihongo|Second assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬允|,''Ten'yaku no jō''}}. * {{nihongo|Alternate assistant to the chief pharmacist|典薬属|,''Ten'yaku no sakan''}}. === Establishment of court Rank === {| class="wikitable" style="font-size:80%; text-align:center; float:right" |- !colspan="2" |{{Nowrap|Rank}} !colspan="2" |Ikai |- !1 |rowspan="2"|一位
first |正一位 |shō ichi-i |- !2 |従一位 |ju ichi-i |- !3 |rowspan="2"|二位
second |正二位 |shō ni-i |- !4 |従二位 |ju ni-i |- !5 |rowspan="2"|三位
third |正三位 |shō san-mi |- !6 |従三位 |ju san-mi |- !7 |rowspan="4"|四位
4th |{{Nowrap|正四位上|1=Senior Fourth Rank|lt=Senior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade}} |shō shi-i no jō |- !8 |正四位下 |shō shi-i no ge |- !9 |従四位上 |ju shi-i no jō |- !10 |従四位下 |ju shi-i no ge |- !11 |rowspan="4"|五位
5th |正五位上 |shō go-i no jō |- !12 |正五位下 |shō go-i no ge |- !13 |従五位上 |ju go-i no jō |- !14 |従五位下 |ju go-i no ge |- !15 |rowspan="4"|六位
6th |正六位上 |shō roku-i no jō |- !16 |正六位下 |shō roku-i no ge |- !17 |従六位上 |ju roku-i no jō |- !18 |従六位下 |ju roku-i no ge |- !19 |rowspan="4"|七位
7th |正七位上 |shō shichi-i no jō |- !20 |正七位下 |shō shichi-i no ge |- !21 |従七位上 |ju shichi-i no jō |- !22 |従七位下 |ju shichi-i no ge |- !23 |rowspan="4"|八位
8th |正八位上 |shō hachi-i no jō |- !24 |正八位下 |shō hachi-i no ge |- !25 |従八位上 |ju hachi-i no jō |- !26 |従八位下 |ju hachi-i no ge |- !27 |rowspan="4"|初位The initial Ranks were subdivided into "greater" (大 ''dai'') and "lesser" (少 ''shō'') Ranks.
initial |大初位上 |dai so-i no jō |- !28 |大初位下 |dai so-i no ge |- !29 |少初位上 |shō so-i no jō |- !30 |少初位下 |shō so-i no ge |} A global system of Ranking for all public posts (官 ''kan'', 官職 ''[[kanshoku]]'') was introduced with over 30 Ranks (位 ''i'', 位階 ''[[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles#Court Ranks|ikai]]''), regulating strictly which posts could be accessed by which Rank. Ranking was supposed to be mostly merit-based, the children of high-Ranking public officials were nonetheless granted a minimal Rank. [[Protected appointments system for hereditary privileges in asia#Japan|This provision]] (蔭位の制 ''on'i no sei'') existed in the Tang law, however under the Japanese ritsuryo Ranks for which it was applied were higher as well as the Ranks obtained by the children. The highest Rank in the system was the first Rank (一位 ''ichi-i''), proceeding downwards to the eighth Rank (八位 ''hachi-i''), held by menials in the court. Below this, an initial Rank called ''so-i'' (初位) existed, but offered few rights.{{cite book | last = Borgen | first = Robert | title = Sugawara no Michizane and the Early Heian Court | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1994 | isbn = 0-8248-1590-4 | pages=13–14}} The top six Ranks were considered true aristocracy (貴 ''ki''), and were subdivided into "senior" (正 ''shō'') and "junior" (従 ''ju'') Ranks (e.g. senior third-Rank [正三位 ''shō san-mi''], junior second-Rank [従二位 ''ju ni-i'']). Below the third Rank, a further subdivision between "upper" (上 ''jō'') and "lower" (下 ''ge'') existed, allowing for Ranks such as “junior fourth Rank lower” (従四位下 ''ju shi-i no ge'') or “senior sixth Rank upper” (正六位上 ''shō roku-i no jō''). Promotion in Ranks was often a very gradual, bureaucratic process, and in the early days of the Codes, one could not advance beyond sixth Rank except by rare exception, thus causing a natural cut-off point between the aristocrats (fifth-Rank and above [貴族 ''kizoku'']) and the menials (sixth-Rank and below [地下 ''jige'']). Additionally, income in the form of ''[[:en:Koku|Koku]]'' (石, 1 koku = about 150 kilograms), or bushels of rice from the provinces, increased dramatically as one advanced in Rank. The average sixth-Rank official might earn 22 koku of rice a year, but the fifth Rank might earn 225 koku of rice, while a third Rank official could earn as much as 6,957 a year. Registration of the citizens (戸籍 ''koseki''), updated every 6 years, and a yearly tax book (計帳 ''keichō'') were established. Based on the ''keichō'', a tax system was established called (租庸調 ''So-yō-chō''). Tax was levied on rice crops but also on several local products (e.g. cotton, salt, tissue) sent to the capital. The system also established local corvée at a provincial level by orders of the [[:en:Kokushi (official)|kokushi]] (国司), a corvée at the Capital (although the corvée at the capital could be replaced by goods sent) and military service. ===Criminal code=== A criminal system was introduced, with {{nihongo|five levels of punishment|五刑|gokei}}. *{{nihongo|[[:en:Caning|Caning]]|笞|chi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 10, 20, 30, 40 or 50 strikes on the buttocks. *{{nihongo|Public caning|杖|jō}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, 60, 70, 80, 90 or 100 strikes on the buttocks, performed in public, using a slightly thicker cane than was used for ''chi''. *{{nihongo|Imprisonment|徒|zu}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, imprisonment for 1, 1.5, 2, 2.5 or 3 years. *[[:en:Exile|Exile]] (流, ''ru'') Depending on the severity of the crime, {{nihongo|nearby exile|近流|''konru''}}, {{nihongo|semi-distant exile|中流|''chūru''}}, or {{nihongo|distant exile|遠流|''onru''}}. *{{nihongo|Death|死|shi}}: Depending on the severity of the crime, death by {{nihongo|hanging|絞|kō}} or {{nihongo|decapitation|斬|zan}}. It defined {{nihongo|eight heavy crimes|八虐|hachigyaku}} that were exempt from amnesty. The code was based on the [[:en:Ten Abominations|Ten Abominations]] of the Tang code, but two crimes related to family life—family discord and disruption of the family (through incest, adultery, etc.) —were removed. ===Handen-Shūju=== In accordance with Chinese legal codes, land as well as citizens were to be "public property" (公地公民). One of the major pillars of the Ritsuryō was the introduction of the '''Handen-Shūju''' (班田収受制) system, similar to the [[:en:Equal-field system|Equal-field system]] in China. The Handen-Shūju regulated land ownership. Based on the registration, each citizen over 6 was entitled to a {{nihongo|"distributed field"|口分田|kubunden}}, subject to taxation (approx. 3% of crops). The area of each field was 2 {{nihongo|''tan''|段}} for men (approx. 22 ares total), and two-thirds of this amount for women. (However, the [[:en:Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō|Shinuhi and Kenin castes]] were only entitled to 1/3 of this area). The field was returned to the country at death. Land belonging to shrines and temples was exempt from taxation. Collection and redistribution of land took place every 6 years. ===Castes=== {{main|Japanese castes under the Ritsuryō}} The population was divided in two castes, ''Ryōmin'' (良民) (furthermore divided into 4 sub-castes{{Citation needed|date=December 2007}}) and ''Senmin'' (賤民) (divided into 5 sub-castes), the latter being close to slaves. Citizens wore different colors according to their caste. ==Evolution of Ritsuryō application== Several modifications were added over time. In order to promote cultivation, a law allowing the ownership for three generations of newly arable fields was promulgated in 723 (三世一身の法, ''Sanze-isshin Law'') and then without limits in 743 (墾田永年私財法, ''Konden Einen Shizai Law''). This led to the appearance of large private lands, the first ''[[shōen]]s''. Strict application of the Handen-Shūju system decayed in the 8th and 9th century.{{Cite book|title=A history of Japan|last=D.|first=Totman, Conrad|date=2000-01-01|publisher=Blackwell Publishers|isbn=1557860769|pages=100|oclc=41967280}} In an attempt to maintain the system, the period between each collection/distribution was extended to 12 years under [[Emperor Kanmu]]. At the beginning of [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]], the system was almost not enforced. The last collection/distribution took place between 902 and 903. The caste system was less and less strictly enforced. Some Ryōmin would wed Senmin to avoid taxation, and Senmin/Ryōmin children would become Ryōmin. At the end of the 9th century / beginning of the 10th, the caste system was practically void of its substance. Hereditary high-Ranks for public posts led to the monopoly of occupation of the most important posts by a limited number of families, in effect a nobility, amongst which the [[Fujiwara clan]], [[Minamoto clan]], [[Taira clan]] and the [[:en:Tachibana clan (kuge)|Tachibana clan]]. ==See also== *{{slink|Law of Japan|Historical developments}} *[[:en:Ōmi Code|Ōmi Code]] *[[:en:Asuka Kiyomihara Code|Asuka Kiyomihara Code]] *[[:en:Taihō Code|Taihō Code]] *[[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] *[[:en:Station bell|Station bell]] *[[:en:Fengjian|Fengjian]] ==Notes== {{reflist}} ==References== * [[:en:Kan'ichi Asakawa|Asakawa]], Kan'ichi. (1903). [https://archive.org/details/bub_gb_K1MuAAAAYAAJ ''The Early Institutional Life of Japan.''] Tokyo: [[:en:Shueisha|Shueisha]]. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/4427686 OCLC 4427686]; [https://openlibrary.org/books/OL6961540M/early_institutional_life_of_Japan ''see'' online, multi-formatted, full-text book at openlibrary.org] * [[:en:Isaac Titsingh|Titsingh, Isaac.]] (1834). ''[[:en:Nihon Odai Ichiran|Nihon Odai Ichiran]]''; ou, [https://books.google.com/books?id=18oNAAAAIAAJ&dq=nipon+o+dai+itsi+ran ''Annales des empereurs du Japon.''] Paris: Royal Asiatic Society, Oriental Translation Fund of Great Britain and Ireland. [https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/5850691 OCLC 5850691] * [[:en:George Sansom|Sansom]], George (1958). ''A History of Japan to 1334.'' Stanford: [[:en:Stanford University Press|Stanford University Press]]. {{ISBN|0-8047-0523-2}} * Haley, John Owen. ''Authority Without Power: Law and the Japanese Paradox'' (Oxford, 1994), {{ISBN|0-19-509257-0}} ==External links== *[https://web.archive.org/web/20150226180044/http://forums.samurai-archives.com/viewtopic.php?t=3397 A full listing of public posts in the Ritsuryō System can be found here] {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Classical Japan]] [[Category:Legal history of Japan]] }} [[en:Ritsuryō]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[de:Ritsuryō]] [[es:Ritsuryō]] [[fa:ریتسوریو]] [[fi:Ritsuryō]] [[fr:Ritsuryō]] [[hr:Ritsuryō]] [[hu:Ricurjó-rendszer]] [[id:Ritsuryō]] [[it:Ritsuryō]] [[ja:律令制]] [[ko:일본의 율령제]] [[nl:Ritsuryo]] [[pt:Ritsuryō]] [[ru:Система рицурё]] [[sr:Рицурјо]] [[th:ริตสึเรียว]] [[tr:Ritsuryō]] [[uk:Система ріцурьо]] [[vi:Ritsuryō]] [[zh:日本律令制]] {{wikidata link|Q742460}} [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]
